result:
There is a specific kind of silence that exists only at four in the morning. It isn’t the heavy, oppressive silence of a locked room, but rather a suspended, breathless quiet. The world is still asleep, and for a brief, stolen window of time, the spinning gears of civilization come to a halt. 

Sitting by the window with a mug of coffee, the darkness outside feels like a thick curtain draped over the neighborhood. Streetlights cast long, amber pools on the empty pavement, unbothered by the rushing tires that will fracture their reflections in just a few hours. This is the liminal space between yesterday and tomorrow—a threshold where time doesn't quite exist. 

I love this hour because it demands nothing. There are no emails to answer, no deadlines looming, no voices asking for attention. It is just the soft hum of the refrigerator, the rhythmic drumming of my own heartbeat, and the slow, creeping arrival of dawn. 

Slowly, the sky begins to bruise with deep indigos and purples, eventually bleeding into soft peaches and grays. The first bird tests its voice—a solitary, trembling note—before the rest of the choir wakes. By the time the sun crests the horizon, the spell is broken. The world rushes back in, loud and demanding. But I have already stolen a piece of the quiet, a secret armor to wear against the noise of the day.
